narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50 and an LSAT score of 35.50, you've clearly put in the effort to build a strong academic foundation, and your upper-middle-class family background reflects stability that many students aspire to. You're planning to attend law school full-time, which is the path most students take. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 57.6%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates with lower predicted failure odds to ensure strong bar passage rates. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance didn't keep pace with peers. This outweighed the positive influences from your LSAT score, which is close to the dataset average of 36.70 and pushed toward a passing prediction, and your above-average undergrad GPA of 3.50, which also helped your case. Your Year 1 decile of 3, lower than the average 5.71, added a moderate nudge toward higher failure risk, while your family income and full-time status had minimal impact. Compared to typical students, your early and late decile rankings stand out as weaker than average, overshadowing your other strengths that align more closely with the norm. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a supportive background—it's the model's data-driven way of flagging patterns where similar profiles faced bar challenges. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average or strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program, then reapply to show improvement. You have the groundwork; targeting those key areas could flip the prediction in your favor next time. We're rooting for you to turn this into a stepping stone toward law school success.
